retrieved_docs.title: Dental Implants -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Implant dentistry the second oldest dental profession; exodontia (oral surgery) is the oldest. Around 600 AD, the Mayan population used pieces of shells as implants to replace mandibular teeth. In 1809, J. Maggiolo inserted a gold implant tube into a fresh extraction site. In 1930, the Strock brothers used Vitallium screws to replace missing teeth. A post-type endosseous implant was developed by Formiggini (the father of modern implantology) and Zepponi in the 1940s. The subperiosteal implant was developed in the 1940s by Dahl in Sweden. In 1946 Strock designed a two-stage screw implant that was inserted without a permucosal post. The abutment post and individual crown were added after this implant completely healed. The desired implant interface at this time was described as ankylosis. In 1967, Dr. Linkow introduced blade implants, now recognized as endosseous implants. Dental implants became a scientific cornerstone after the serendipitous invention of Dr. Branemark who helped in the evolution of the concept of osseointegration (direct, rigid attachment of the implant to the bone without any intervening tissue in between two implants) [1] [2] [3] .

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Implants -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The armamentarium (comprising equipment and different parts of an implant) used for the surgical placement of a dental implant is as follows: Disposable syringes Disposable surgical blades Towel clips Periosteal elevator Dental implant surgical kit (containing different drills) Physiodispenser with the surgical handpiece Dental implant Cover screw Healing abutment Needle holder Toothed tissue holding forceps Scissors, surgical sutures (in flap surgery) Soft tissue punch (for flapless surgeries) Types of implants and implant materials Endosteal Subperiosteal Transosteal

retrieved_docs.title: Orthopedic Implant Materials -- Issues of Concern -- Surgically Applicable Machining Properties
retrieved_docs.content: Smoothness is a quantifiable material property widely that is important in bearing design. Many early total knee designs, including the Miller-Galante, had a titanium surface. While these M-G implants were very successful in their time, wear characteristics of the titanium were supplanted by "smoother" metals (like cobalt-chrome alloys). Titanium is unable to be polished and machined to a surface smoothness competitive with other implant materials such as cobalt chrome. Modern total knee implants are mostly titanium alloy at the bone interface and cobalt chrome alloys at the load-bearing surface. This property allows for favorable bone-implant interface (titanium) and desirable smoothness for load-bearing (cobalt-chrome). Titanium plates and other implants are readily worked in the operating room and in-vivo. Implant benders, burs, and cutting devices are common tools used to customize implants to specific needs.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Chin implants are inserted either intraorally or submentally. An intraoral incision is made in the anterior mandibular vestibule, spanning from canine to canine. The incision is made with a blade or bovie electrocautery device through the mucosa until the mentalis muscle is visualized. The muscle bellies can be cut with a bovie electrocautery device, scissors, or a blade. A cuff of mentalis muscle should be left attached to the mandible and lip.

retrieved_docs.title: Pectoral Implants -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Surgeons will often have multiple sizes and shapes of implants available. For the surgical procedure, a blunt dissector is necessary to develop a sub-pectoral plane for implant placement. Otherwise, a standard surgical suite and instrumentation are adequate for this procedure. Drains are not standard and are not needed. [3] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Osseointegrated Bone-Conducting Hearing Protheses -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The countersink drill is performed in the same fashion as above until the countersink is created at a depth of 0.5 mm; avoid making the hole larger than it is as it may risk implant instability. Proceed to place implant and abutment by first setting the drill to low speed 15-30 rpm with adequate torque drill setting 20-40 Ncm (Newton centimeter). Then proceed to attach the hand-piece to the abutment inserter; this will be used to retrieve the abutment-implant device. It is important not to contaminate the implant while retrieving or placing it in the skull. Place implant-abutment and rotate until the adequate depth is reached (drill engine will stop at adequate depth). Remove handpiece and abutment inserter gently. Wound closure is achieved with 4-0 vicryl for the deep layers of skin and is followed by a running 5-0 fast absorbing plain gut. Perform a punch biopsy (5mm) in skin overlying abutment. Once the abutment is exposed, attach a healing cap with a plug. Then place a thin dressing cover in bacitracin ointment below the healing cap. Finally, cover the healing cap with a mastoid dressing.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: During the procedure, intravenous (IV) deep or general anesthesia induction can help attain the desired anesthesia depth. Local analgesia is then obtained using lidocaine and a longer-lasting anesthetic like marcaine. Local anesthetics are usually drawn from a vial and administered with a dental syringe and a 27-gauge needle. A #15 blade or bovie electrocautery is used for the appropriate incision. The surgical armamentarium is similar regardless of the implant location and type. The table below summarizes the materials and equipment used for various aspects of the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthopedic Implant Materials -- Issues of Concern -- Surgically Applicable Machining Properties
retrieved_docs.content: Stainless steel can be polished to a relatively high smoothness and has been/is in use in many arthroplasty applications. Stainless steel plates are ductile enough to be altered in the operative theatre in many situations.  Benders, burrs, and cutting tools are commonly available to alter this material in the operative theatre.

retrieved_docs.title: Osseointegrated Bone-Conducting Hearing Protheses -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There are two types of conventional hearing aids; air conduction hearing aids and bone conduction hearing aids. Air conductive hearing aids are a medical device that consists of a microphone, amplifier, and transducer that works as a loudspeaker in the external auditory canal. Bone conductive hearing aids consist and function similarly to air conductive hearing aids but differ in how the transducer function and where it is placed. In this type of hearing aids, the transducer work as bone oscillators that transmits vibration to the inner ear from the sound recollected by the external microphone. To effectively transduce the sound into a vibration, a medical device needs to be located near the temporal bone. The hearing aid is often placed near the temporal bone using headband or eyeglasses pieces. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Osseointegrated Bone-Conducting Hearing Protheses -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Equipment required for surgical implantation of osseointegrated bone conduction prosthesis includes but is not limited to: Marker 27 gauge needle Hemostat Ruler A 10 ml syringe with 1% Lidocaine with epinephrine 1:100,000 Blade 15 with the blade holder Bipolar cautery Self Retractors Surgical instruments: Dummy device, raspatorium, dissector, drill, drill indicator, torque wrenches, screwdriver, and abutment inserter Company-specific drill console Drilling disposable: guide drill and countersink drill (3-4 mm widening). Implants: Abutment and Fixture (multiple length and width provided by the individual manufacturer). Punch Biopsy (measurement 5 mm) 4-0 Vicryl suture 5-0 fast absorbing plain gut suture Thin nonadherent dressing Bacitracin antibiotic ointment Healing cap

retrieved_docs.title: Osseointegrated Bone-Conducting Hearing Protheses -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: A vertical incision is performed at the skin with a 15-blade scalpel down to the periosteum; it is important to limit bipolar cauterization in this step. Self-retractors are used to facilitate periosteal exposure. When adequate exposure is achieved, perform cruciate incision at periosteum and raise the edge with raspatorium to expose approximately 6 mm of bone. After obtaining adequate bone exposure for implant, proceed to drill a fixture guide hole. First, attach drill bit to 3mm spacer drill. Then set up the manufacturer drill unit to a high-speed setting of 2,000 rpm. Proceed to place the guide drill in a perpendicular position. Then drill the fixture hole to 3 mm depth; verify that there is bone underneath the drill site and constantly irrigate to avoid osteocyte injury. After 3mm depth is reached, make sure no dura has been exposed; remove the spacer-attachment on the 3-mm guide drill and continue to drill in the same fashion until 4 mm depth is reached. Once adequate implant depth is reached, proceed to use the widening drill, also known as countersink drill, to widen the area of the implant. Before drilling is begun, revise that the drilling unit is at high speed 2,000 rpm.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Mini-Implants -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative planning includes a maximum of diagnostic information. A panoramic x-ray is a minimum requirement, a Cone Beam CT scan is recommended for 3D planning especially in cases with very narrow ridges. Raising a flap or flapless; If there is sufficient width of the ridge a flapless transgingival technique for the pilot drill is possible. When however a narrow ridge of extensive soft tissue is present a minimal flap (crestal incision) is recommended to reveal the bone. This would allow the exact placement of the implants at the correct angulation in the bone. The mini dental implant system utilizes a self-tapping threaded screw design and employs minimally invasive surgical intervention. For successful osteointegration and high success rate, initial stability of the mini implant is important and is dependent upon the bone quality, implant design, and surgical technique used. [1] Failure of the mini implant-supported maxillary removable prosthesis is attributed to the facial angulation of maxillary implants, thick masticatory mucosa requiring longer implant abutments, and disparallelism of the unsplinted implants producing micromovements. [14] Retention rings of the mini implant-supported overdenture should be periodically changed. [1] A torque of 35 N/cm is used during implantation of the mini dental implants with a ratchet. [1] It is important to reline the mini implant-supported removable complete prosthesis periodically and to perform occlusal adjustments for appropriate force and movement distribution to avoid implant fracture and overload-induced bone loss around the implants. [15] [16] [17] Implant placement involves the following procedure: The left and right mental foramen are marked with an intra-oral skin marker. The ridge is marked 7 mm anterior of the mental foramen to indicate the most distal implant size. This safety zone includes a potentially present 3 to 5 mm anterior loop and a 2 mm security margin.

retrieved_docs.title: Minimally Invasive Aortic Valve Surgery -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The following is a summary of the different types of aortic valves that are currently available for surgical placement: Bioprosthetic valves (porcine or bovine): Stented - Perimount (Edwards), Epic (St Jude Medical), Hancock II (Medtronic) Stented, supraannular position - Magna Ease (Edwards), Mosaic (Medtronic) Stented, externally mounted leaflets - Mitroflow (Sorin), Trifecta (St. Jude Medical) Stentless - Freedom (Sorin), Toronto SPV (St Jude Medical), Freestyle (Medtronic), PrimaPlus (Edwards) Mechanical valves: Regent (St Jude Medical) - bileaflet Single-tilting disc (Medtronic-Hall) - no longer used Caged-ball (Starr-Edwards) - historically used, original mechanical valve Tilting-disc (Björk-Shiley & Pfizer) - no longer used

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Implants -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Endosteal implants pierce only one cortical plate of maxilla and mandible. The most frequently used endosteal implant is root form implant. The subperiosteal implant has an implant substructure and superstructure where custom cast frame is placed directly beneath the periosteum. Transosteal implant crosses through both cortical plates. Implants can be divided into three materials: Metals Ceramics Polymers There are three biologic responses when implants are rooted in the host tissue: Biotorerant: surrounded by fibrous tissue, e.g., stainless steel Bioinert: direct rigid attachment, e.g., titanium and its alloys Bioactive: allows the formation of bone on their surface, e.g., hydroxyapatite [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Implantable Hearing Devices -- Technique or Treatment -- Percutaneous Implants
retrieved_docs.content: If the soft tissue thickness was measured to be more than 10 mm, thinning of the soft tissue over the implant site is performed. The abutment is mounted on the implant. The drill is decreased to a rate of thirty to forty rpm, and the implant is placed again with adequate irrigation to avoid thermal injury to the bone. The implant is tightened to 25 newtons. The soft tissue is then draped over the implant, and a 5 mm skin punch is performed to exteriorize the abutment. The incision is then closed according to the surgeon's preference. A pre-manufactured cap is usually attached to the abutment when it is left in place for one week.

retrieved_docs.title: Ankle Arthroplasty -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: More modern third-generation implants have been designed to counter these causes of failure. [9] Examples of third-generation TAA implants include the Salto (Tornier SA, Saint Ismier, France), Hintegra (Newdeal SA, Lyon), Mobility (DePuy, Warsaw, IN), and Bologna-Oxford (BOX; Finsbury Orthopaedics, Leatherhead, Surrey, UK). These implants use a standalone polyethylene component not built into the tibial or talar components. They require minimal bone resection; however, they do require adequate balancing for implant success. These implants rely heavily on the integrity of the ligamentous structures surrounding the ankle. As implant technology has advanced, implant instrumentation has been refined, often incorporating cutting jigs. Preoperative computed tomography planning and patient-specific instrumentation in 3-dimensional printed cutting guides have evolved to reduce operative time and fluoroscopic exposure without compromising implant position. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 230
retrieved_docs.content: hip biomechanics. Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, the radius and ulna, and other lower‑extremity sites, underscoring its role across multiple skeletal regions. Collectively, these entities illustrate an integrated framework for fracture fixation, deformity correction, growth modulation, prosthetic revision, and preventive care in thigh and leg surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 164
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category aggregates a wide array of operative interventions that target the femur, tibia, fibula, hip, and associated joint structures. Direct femoral procedures include internal fixation of the femur (with or without fracture reduction), intramedullary nailing, open reduction of femur fractures (with or without internal fixation), suture of the periosteum of the femur, repair of distal femur malunion, femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and femoral shortening performed with a blade plate. Osteoplastic techniques—osteoplasty of the femur, femoral lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with segment transfer—address limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. Growth‑modulating strategies comprise epiphysiodesis of the distal femur (and proximal tibia or fibula) and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia, or fibula for correction of genu varus or valgus. Reconstructive

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 154
retrieved_docs.content: of the femur includes “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” which provide stabilization through plates, screws, rods, or combined reduction and fixation. Bone‑shaping and length‑altering procedures—“Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer”—modify femoral geometry to treat limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. “Prophylactic treatment of femur” and “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck” aim to prevent pathology or correct malunited fractures, while “Suture of periosteum of femur” supports periosteal integrity for bone healing. Prosthetic and joint‑related interventions involve “Replacement of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: The procedural category **THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS** encompasses a comprehensive set of operative interventions that address fractures, dislocations, deformities, limb‑length discrepancies, prosthetic failures, and reconstructive needs of the hip, femur, tibia, fibula, and adjacent lower‑extremity structures. Direct‑site femoral procedures include **internal fixation of femur** (with or without fracture reduction), **intramedullary nailing of femur**, **open reduction of fracture of femur** (with or without internal fixation), **femoral shortening with blade plate**, **repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck**, and various **osteoplasties of femur** (plain, lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with femoral segment transfer). **Trochanterplasty** and **femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ)** are also performed directly on the femur. Growth‑plate modulation techniques such as **arrest, epiphyseal, any method

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1223
retrieved_docs.content: The procedures of measuring and adjusting an unspecified external prosthesis, as well as the measurement of an arm prosthesis, are categorized within rehabilitation care that encompasses the fitting of prostheses and the adjustment of medical devices. This classification highlights that both activities are integral components of prosthetic rehabilitation, aimed at ensuring proper device fit and functionality for optimal patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1180
retrieved_docs.content: Transcatheter aortic valve implantation, aortic valve replacement, mitral (crown) valve replacement, pulmonary valve replacement, and tricuspid valve repair are all specific types of heart‑valve procedures, each representing a narrower category within the broader group of cardiac valve operations. Valve stenosis, although a pathological condition rather than a technique, is closely related to these procedures because it is a common indication for surgical or transcatheter valve intervention. Right‑heart catheterization employs vascular catheters, and the same vascular catheters serve as the access devices for performing transcatheter aortic valve implantation. This interconnected use of catheters highlights the shared technological platform that underlies both diagnostic right‑heart catheterization and therapeutic transcatheter valve implantation, emphasizing the importance of catheter‑based approaches in modern cardiac valve care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: A wide range of orthopedic interventions are linked to the femur, reflecting the bone’s central role in lower‑extremity surgery. The procedure described as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” is associated with the femur, indicating that epiphyseal arrest techniques may involve the distal femur in conjunction with the proximal tibia and fibula. Direct surgical actions on the femur include “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” each of which specifies the femur as the direct procedure site. Procedures that are performed on or modify the femur more generally are identified as having the femur as the procedure site, such as “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Interventions that involve the femur in a less direct manner are classified with an indirect relationship, exemplified by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Suture of periosteum of femur,” both of which denote the femur as an indirect procedure site. Together, these relationships delineate how various surgical techniques—ranging from fracture fixation and bone shortening to prosthetic revision and soft‑tissue repair—are organized around the femur, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding femoral‑focused orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: heart‑valve and related cardiac procedures, clarifies the procedural methods and devices employed (such as clinical insertion techniques and vascular catheters), and highlights the interconnections between broader procedural categories and their specific clinical applications, which is essential for accurate classification, planning, and delivery of cardiac patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: داخلي للكسر) is likewise a member of the osteoporosis/low‑bone‑density group and is applied across diverse skeletal sites, including the clavicle, hip, lower extremities (other than hip or femur), and the radius and ulna. Procedures and conditions related to osteoporosis extend beyond fracture fixation; hip‑head prosthesis (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are also members of the osteoporosis SMQ, illustrating how severe bone‑density loss can lead to joint replacement and recurrent fractures. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that clavicular and shoulder‑arm surgical repairs are precisely linked to shoulder anatomy, that femoral intracapsular fractures correspond to hip neck fractures and are described in multiple languages, and that the classification of closed fractures and various fixation techniques is tightly connected to osteoporosis‑related bone fragility. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: of closed fractures and various fixation techniques is tightly connected to osteoporosis‑related bone fragility. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical planning, and coordinated management of fractures and bone‑density disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: conditions. Collectively, the knowledge base connects clavicular surgery to a comprehensive shoulder‑and‑arm operative framework, aligns closed‑fracture management with underlying osteoporosis, and integrates internal fixation and hip replacement techniques across multiple skeletal regions, thereby supporting coordinated orthopedic decision‑making and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 979
retrieved_docs.content: The category THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS, which is classified under the broader treatment of fracture or dislocation of the lower extremity (other than hip or femur), comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions. These interventions include arrest, epiphyseal, any method (e.g., epiphysiodesis) of the distal femur; arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (e.g., for genu varus or valgus); femoral shortening with blade plate; internal fixation of femur; open reduction of fracture of femur; osteoplasty of femur, lengthening; prophylactic treatment of femur; replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate; and revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure). Together, these procedures represent the surgical repertoire for addressing complex thigh and leg injuries, deformities, and prosthetic revisions, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate reparative and reconstructive strategies for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category is a comprehensive treatment modality for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur as well as for lower‑extremity injuries that do not involve the hip or femur. Within this framework a full spectrum of femoral procedures is organized as child entities, including growth‑modulating techniques such as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus).” Direct operative interventions on the femur comprise “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” “Osteoplasty of femur,”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: (excluding hip or femur), and fracture or dislocation of the radius and ulna—and is an inverse‑ISA of “internal fixation of fracture” (تثبيت داخلي للكسر). A specialized form, **internal fixation of the clavicle without fracture reduction**, is also included. The “internal fixation of fracture” concept itself is a member of the osteoporosis/low‑bone‑density SMQ, reinforcing the association between osteoporotic bone and the need for fixation even when reduction is not performed. In the same SMQ, the concepts **إعاضة رأس الفخذ** (hip‑head prosthesis) and **كسور متعددة** (multiple fractures) are identified, underscoring that patients with reduced bone quality are prone to multiple fractures and may require prosthetic reconstruction of the femoral head. Collectively, these relationships illustrate an integrated framework in which surgical repair and fixation techniques for clavicular, humeral, radial, ulnar, scapular, and femoral injuries are organized under a shoulder‑arm reconstruction

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 191
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the taxonomy integrates therapeutic valve surgeries, diagnostic catheterizations, and biopsy techniques, clarifying their hierarchical relationships, device associations, and clinical contexts for coordinated patient care.
